front: Poem: addressed to a "wee, sleeket, cowran, tim'rous beastie"; contains "the best laid schemes o' Mice an' Men gang aft agley"
Poem: protagonist encounters "warlocks and witches in a dance" on his ride home; his mare Meg loses her tail to the witch Nannie at the River Doon
Poem: speaker pledges love "while the sands of life shall run" and compares it to a flower "newly sprung in June"
Poem: calls haggis the "chieftain o' the puddin' race"; recited at celebrations on January 25th
Poem: "O would some Power the gift give us / To see ourselves as others see us!" — about a creature on a lady's bonnet at church
Poem: "gin a body meet a body" is misquoted by Holden Caulfield, providing the title of The Catcher in the Rye
